stem:
A 4-week-old infant presents to your emergency department with a 5-day history of progressive coughing fits. His mother describes bouts in which 'he goes red, then blue around the lips, then takes a deep noisy breath in and is exhausted'. He has fed poorly during these bouts. His sister had a cough 3 weeks ago and was told she had a viral chest infection. His mother did not receive the antenatal pertussis booster.

On examination he is afebrile and settled between paroxysms. There are bilateral subconjunctival haemorrhages and an audible inspiratory whoop is observed after one cough cluster in the department. The chest is clear on auscultation.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 168, BP 78/46 mmHg, RR 56 (paroxysmal bursts to 80), SpO2 nadir 84% during paroxysm and 96% at rest, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Subconjunctival haemorrhages bilaterally, inspiratory whoop, chest clear to auscultation, fontanelle soft, no rash
data_table.Investigations: Total white cell count 32 x10^9/L (5.0-19.5), lymphocyte 22 x10^9/L (3.0-9.5), CRP 6 mg/L (<5), chest X-ray clear, nasopharyngeal swab PCR pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure the family, give paracetamol for comfort, and discharge home with cough safety-net advice
options.rationale: Infants under 6 months with paroxysmal cough, whoop, and desaturations require admission for monitored apnoea risk; discharge underestimates progression and contact-tracing duty. (Source: CDNA SoNG Pertussis)

options.text: Admit for cardiorespiratory monitoring, commence azithromycin orally for 5 days, and apply droplet precautions
options.rationale: Infant pertussis with desaturation, lymphocytosis, and household exposure mandates admission for apnoea observation; azithromycin shortens infectivity and is preferred under 1 month over erythromycin (pyloric stenosis risk reduced). Droplet precautions plus public health notification are required. (Source: CDNA SoNG Pertussis; RCH CPG: Pertussis)

options.text: Discharge on oral amoxicillin and arrange general practitioner review for an audible wheeze in 48 hours
options.rationale: Amoxicillin has no activity against Bordetella pertussis; macrolides are required. There is also no wheeze documented. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Trial nebulised salbutamol and ipratropium and admit to the short-stay observation unit overnight
options.rationale: Beta-agonists do not modify pertussis paroxysms; bronchodilators are reserved for documented reactive airways. Short-stay observation is also inappropriate for an infant under 6 months with apnoea risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

options.text: Commence empirical intravenous ceftriaxone for unrecognised early-onset neonatal bacterial sepsis
options.rationale: Sepsis screen is reasonable in unwell infants under 28 days, but afebrile lymphocytosis with whoop after exposure favours pertussis; ceftriaxone is also contraindicated under 1 month if jaundiced or receiving calcium and is not the targeted treatment. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis assessment and management)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Sepsis assessment and management

explanation.summary: Pertussis in infants under 6 months has high apnoea, pneumonia, and death rates. A characteristic triad of paroxysmal cough, inspiratory whoop, and post-tussive emesis or apnoea, with marked lymphocytosis, is enough to start macrolides empirically while PCR returns. Azithromycin is preferred under 1 month given infant pyloric stenosis risk reported with erythromycin. Notification to public health and post-exposure prophylaxis for close contacts (including non-immunised household members) is required. Antenatal maternal vaccination at 20-32 weeks reduces severe infant disease.
key_points: Admit any infant under 6 months with proven or suspected pertussis for apnoea monitoring
Azithromycin preferred under 1 month (pyloric stenosis risk with erythromycin)
Notifiable disease - contact tracing and prophylaxis for vulnerable contacts
Antenatal maternal booster at 20-32 weeks is the primary prevention strategy
explanation.pearls: Azithromycin 10 mg/kg orally once daily for 5 days is the AU first-line for treatment and post-exposure prophylaxis in infants under 1 month.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Sepsis assessment and management

stem:
A previously well 22-month-old boy presents 90 minutes after a sudden coughing and choking episode at his grandmother's house. He had been eating a handful of peanuts on the lounge floor. His mother says 'he turned bright red, coughed for a few minutes, and now seems alright but is breathing a bit oddly'. He has no fever, no preceding cough, and no asthma history.

On examination he is alert, mildly tachypnoeic, and speaks in full short sentences. The trachea is central. There is reduced air entry over the right lower zone with a localised monophonic wheeze; the left chest is clear. A chest X-ray in inspiration looks essentially normal; expiratory and right lateral decubitus films show hyperinflation of the right lower lobe and mediastinal shift away from that side.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 124, BP 96/60 mmHg, RR 38, SpO2 96% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert, no stridor, central trachea, reduced air entry right lower zone, localised monophonic right-sided wheeze, no drooling
data_table.Investigations: Inspiratory chest X-ray reported normal; expiratory and right lateral decubitus views show right lower lobe hyperinflation with mediastinal shift away from the affected side

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol and a short course of oral prednisolone, with respiratory clinic review in two weeks
options.rationale: Beta-agonists and steroids are appropriate for asthma but do not address a retained foreign body, and delay risks distal infection and granulation. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign body inhalation)

options.text: Five back blows and five chest thrusts in head-down position, then admit for overnight observation
options.rationale: Back blows are reserved for the conscious choking child with complete airway obstruction; this child is settled, talking, and oxygenating. The retained foreign body still needs definitive removal. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 4)

options.text: Refer urgently for rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthetic for retrieval of the foreign body
options.rationale: Witnessed choking with localised wheeze, focal reduced air entry, and expiratory hyperinflation with mediastinal shift on the right is a classic ball-valve foreign body. Rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthetic by an experienced ENT or paediatric airway team is the diagnostic and therapeutic intervention; flexible bronchoscopy is diagnostic only. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign body inhalation)

options.text: Order a computed tomography scan of the chest before deciding on further airway management
options.rationale: Most aspirated organic foreign bodies are radiolucent and CT can be falsely reassuring; the diagnosis is clinical and the next step is bronchoscopy. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign body inhalation)

options.text: Empirical oral amoxicillin-clavulanate and outpatient chest physiotherapy with review in three days
options.rationale: Antibiotics and physiotherapy are not the answer to a retained foreign body and do not relieve obstruction; missed foreign bodies lead to post-obstructive pneumonia and bronchiectasis. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign body inhalation)

explanation.summary: Inhaled foreign body in toddlers presents as a witnessed choking episode followed by an asymptomatic period before localised signs reappear. Peanuts and dry pulses are the most frequent culprits. Expiratory or decubitus films demonstrate air trapping from a ball-valve effect; a normal inspiratory film does not exclude the diagnosis. Definitive management is rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthetic, and the index of suspicion - not imaging - drives the referral.
key_points: Witnessed choke plus focal monophonic wheeze is bronchoscopy until proven otherwise
Normal inspiratory CXR does not exclude foreign body
Rigid bronchoscopy is diagnostic and therapeutic - flexible is diagnostic only
Peanuts under 3 years - dietary education at every well-child check
explanation.pearls: On expiratory or lateral decubitus chest X-ray, the obstructed side stays hyperinflated and the mediastinum shifts away - the opposite of the side you might expect.

stem:
A 14-year-old boy presents to your emergency department with 2 hours of sudden severe left-sided scrotal pain that woke him at 5 am. He has vomited twice. He says 'it started straight away, not a slow build, and it hurts down to my belly button'. There is no dysuria, no urethral discharge, no recent trauma, and he denies sexual activity.

On examination he is in obvious distress. The left hemiscrotum is swollen and erythematous. The left testis is exquisitely tender, lies higher than the right, and is held horizontally. The cremasteric reflex is absent on the left and present on the right. There is no penile discharge. A bedside scrotal ultrasound is available but would take 90 minutes to arrange with formal Doppler reporting.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 104, BP 124/76 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.9 °C
data_table.Examination: Left testis tender, high-riding, horizontal lie, absent cremasteric reflex; right side normal; no penile discharge; abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis blood and leukocytes negative; FBC and inflammatory markers not yet returned

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Proceed directly to surgical scrotal exploration without waiting for ultrasound imaging
options.rationale: Classic torsion features (sudden pain, absent cremasteric reflex, high-riding horizontal lie, vomiting) within the salvage window of about 6 hours mandate immediate exploration; imaging that delays surgery reduces salvage rates. Contralateral fixation is performed at the same operation. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute scrotal pain; Urological Society of Australia and New Zealand)
source_refs: Urological Society of Australia and New Zealand

options.text: Arrange formal scrotal colour Doppler ultrasound before any decision about operative exploration
options.rationale: Doppler can miss intermittent or partial torsion and any imaging delay reduces salvage; clinical features here are diagnostic. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute scrotal pain)

options.text: Commence empirical intravenous ceftriaxone and oral doxycycline for presumed epididymo-orchitis
options.rationale: Epididymo-orchitis usually has a more gradual onset, dysuria, preserved cremasteric reflex, and Prehn sign relief on elevation; antibiotic empirical cover here delays surgery. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Attempt outward manual detorsion at the bedside as definitive treatment with discharge afterwards
options.rationale: Manual detorsion can temporise pain in delayed presentations while theatre is readied but is never definitive; orchidopexy is still required. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute scrotal pain)

options.text: Administer intramuscular morphine and observe for 6 hours with repeat examination at intervals
options.rationale: Watchful waiting risks irreversible ischaemia; the window to salvage is approximately 6 hours from onset. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute scrotal pain)

explanation.summary: Testicular torsion is a time-critical diagnosis. Sudden severe scrotal pain, vomiting, an absent cremasteric reflex, and a high-riding, horizontal testis is sufficient clinical evidence to proceed to theatre without ultrasound. Salvage rates fall sharply after 6 hours of ischaemia; bilateral orchidopexy is performed at exploration because the contralateral side shares the bell-clapper anatomy. Differentials (epididymo-orchitis, torsion of testicular appendage, idiopathic scrotal oedema) typically have a more gradual onset and preserved cremasteric reflex.
key_points: Torsion is a clinical diagnosis - never delay surgery for imaging
Salvage falls after about 6 hours of ischaemia
Bilateral orchidopexy performed at exploration (bell-clapper is bilateral)
Absent cremasteric reflex is the most specific bedside sign
explanation.pearls: Sudden-onset scrotal pain in a teenage boy with an absent cremasteric reflex goes straight to theatre - ultrasound is for equivocal cases, not classic ones.

sources.label: Urological Society of Australia and New Zealand

stem:
A 5-day-old, term, exclusively breastfed boy is brought to the early-discharge clinic by his mother who says 'he looks more yellow today and is bringing his feeds up'. Birth weight 3.4 kg, now 3.05 kg (10% below birth weight). He was discharged at 28 hours of age with normal newborn screening swab pending and a normal transcutaneous bilirubin at 24 hours. His mother is blood group O Rhesus positive; baby's blood group is not yet known. Latch has been variable and feeds are short.

On examination he is sleepy but rousable, with jaundice extending to mid-thigh. There is no hepatosplenomegaly and the neurological exam is normal. Total serum bilirubin is 360 micromol/L with a conjugated fraction of 8 micromol/L. The phototherapy nomogram threshold for a well term infant at this postnatal age and risk profile is 340 micromol/L; the exchange transfusion threshold is 480 micromol/L. Direct antiglobulin test, baby's blood group, FBC, and reticulocyte count have been sent and remain pending.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 148, BP 72/44 mmHg, RR 44, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Visible jaundice to mid-thigh, sleepy but rousable, soft fontanelle, normal tone, no hepatosplenomegaly, mild dry mucous membranes
data_table.Investigations: Total serum bilirubin 360 micromol/L, conjugated bilirubin 8 micromol/L; phototherapy threshold 340 micromol/L; exchange threshold 480 micromol/L; DAT, blood group, FBC, reticulocyte count pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure the mother, advise more frequent feeds, and review with repeat bilirubin in 24 hours
options.rationale: The total serum bilirubin already exceeds the phototherapy threshold; waiting another 24 hours risks rapid escalation toward exchange and acute bilirubin encephalopathy. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

options.text: Arrange immediate exchange transfusion in a neonatal intensive care unit setting today
options.rationale: Total bilirubin is well below the exchange threshold of 480 micromol/L and there are no signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy; exchange transfusion carries significant procedural risks. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

options.text: Discontinue breastfeeding for 48 hours and switch to a standard infant formula by bottle
options.rationale: Breastfeeding cessation is not first-line; phototherapy plus continued breastfeeding with lactation support is the AU standard. Cessation undermines establishment and is rarely required. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

options.text: Admit for intensive phototherapy and continue breastfeeding with lactation support and feed top-ups
options.rationale: Bilirubin above the phototherapy line at this postnatal age requires admission for intensive phototherapy. Breastfeeding should continue with lactation support and expressed breast milk or formula top-ups to address suboptimal feeding contributing to dehydration and weight loss; DAT and group results guide whether haemolysis is contributing. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice; Australian College of Midwives Position Statement)
source_refs: Australian College of Midwives Position Statement

options.text: Commence intravenous immunoglobulin empirically before the direct antiglobulin test result returns
options.rationale: IVIG is indicated for confirmed isoimmune haemolytic disease with rising bilirubin near exchange threshold despite phototherapy, not empirically before serology. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

explanation.summary: Neonatal jaundice management is plotted on age- and risk-adjusted nomograms. A value above the phototherapy line at day 5 in a well infant warrants admission for intensive phototherapy and continued breastfeeding with adequate feeding support. Conjugated bilirubin over 15% of total (or over 20 micromol/L) raises concern for biliary atresia and prompts urgent paediatric assessment. Acute bilirubin encephalopathy features (hypertonia, opisthotonus, high-pitched cry, lethargy refractory to feeding) demand immediate exchange.
key_points: Plot bilirubin on age-and-risk nomogram - thresholds are time-dependent
Continue breastfeeding with support during phototherapy where possible
Conjugated > 15% of total or > 20 micromol/L: think biliary atresia, urgent referral
Send DAT, group, FBC, reticulocyte count when above phototherapy line
explanation.pearls: Severe weight loss (>10% of birth weight by day 5) plus jaundice usually reflects insufficient breast milk intake; lactation support is essential alongside phototherapy.

sources.label: Australian College of Midwives Position Statement

stem:
A 6-year-old girl is reviewed in your general paediatric outpatient clinic after a 3-day history of a non-blanching rash on her buttocks and lower legs. Two days before the rash she had bilateral ankle swelling and pain. Her mother says 'she's been gripping her tummy when it cramps, but it goes off after a minute and she's eating'. There is no haematemesis, melaena, or testicular pain. She had a sore throat 10 days ago that settled without antibiotics.

On examination she is well and afebrile, with palpable purpura on the buttocks, posterior thighs, and shins. Joints are not warm and ranges are full. The abdomen is soft with mild central tenderness, no peritonism, and audible bowel sounds. Blood pressure is normal. Urinalysis shows 1+ blood and trace protein; spot urine protein-to-creatinine ratio is 18 mg/mmol (normal <20). Renal function, FBC, and coagulation are within normal limits.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 96, BP 102/64 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Palpable purpura buttocks and lower limbs, joints non-swollen, abdomen soft, mild central tenderness, no peritonism, no testicular involvement
data_table.Investigations: FBC and platelet count within normal limits, coagulation profile normal, creatinine 38 micromol/L, urinalysis blood 1+ trace protein, urine protein:creatinine ratio 18 mg/mmol (<20)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit for intravenous methylprednisolone pulses and renal biopsy within the next 48 hours
options.rationale: Methylprednisolone pulses and biopsy are reserved for nephritic-range proteinuria, declining renal function, or biopsy-proven crescentic disease; this child has mild urinalysis findings only. (Source: KHA-CARI; RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for the cutaneous purpura and review in one week
options.rationale: Steroids do not alter the natural history of purpura or prevent renal involvement; they are reserved for severe abdominal pain, orchitis, or established nephritis. Routine prescription overtreats. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Commence empirical oral amoxicillin for 10 days given the recent sore throat as a presumed trigger
options.rationale: Antibiotics do not modify the post-infectious vasculitis course; treat any concurrent group A streptococcal pharyngitis on its own merits, not prophylactically. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Refer urgently to paediatric nephrology for an immediate inpatient renal biopsy this admission
options.rationale: Biopsy is indicated for nephrotic-range proteinuria, hypertension, declining renal function, or persistent significant proteinuria beyond 3 months, not for mild transient urinary changes. (Source: KHA-CARI)

options.text: Discharge home with analgesia, written safety-net advice, and weekly urinalysis and BP checks for 6 months
options.rationale: Mild IgA vasculitis with normal blood pressure, normal renal function, and a urine protein:creatinine ratio under 20 mg/mmol is managed conservatively. Ongoing surveillance is essential because nephritis can declare itself up to 6 months after rash onset and dictates referral. Written advice covers when to return (severe abdominal pain, testicular pain, frank haematuria, hypertension). (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura; KHA-CARI)

explanation.summary: IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schonlein purpura) is the commonest childhood vasculitis. Most cases are self-limiting with palpable purpura, arthralgia, abdominal pain, and microscopic haematuria. Management is supportive analgesia and longitudinal urinalysis plus blood pressure surveillance for 6 months because renal involvement can present late. Steroids are reserved for severe gut pain, orchitis, or proven nephritis. Refer to nephrology for nephrotic-range proteinuria, declining function, or hypertension.
key_points: Steroids do not prevent nephritis - reserve for severe gut, orchitis, biopsy-proven disease
Surveillance for 6 months: weekly urinalysis and BP, then taper
Refer for nephrotic-range proteinuria, hypertension, or rising creatinine
Most arthralgia and skin disease settles within 4 weeks
explanation.pearls: Urine protein:creatinine ratio over 250 mg/mmol or nephritic syndrome features within 6 months of HSP onset warrants paediatric nephrology referral for biopsy consideration.

stem:
An 18-month-old girl is brought to the emergency department after a 90-second generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home. She had been febrile to 39.4 °C since waking that morning with a runny nose. The seizure stopped before paramedics arrived; she is now alert, taking fluids, and back to her usual play. Her mother says 'she had one of these six months ago when she had tonsillitis and we were told it was a febrile fit, but I'm worried it's becoming a habit'.

Family history is unremarkable; there is no family history of epilepsy. Development is age-appropriate, immunisations are up to date, and the most recent seizure description matches a typical simple febrile seizure. Neurological examination is normal. The chest is clear, tympanic membranes are pink without effusion, and the throat is mildly erythematous without exudate.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 132, BP 96/58 mmHg, RR 28, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 38.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert, playful, soft fontanelle closed, normal tone, brisk symmetrical reflexes, plantars downgoing, no neck stiffness, mildly erythematous oropharynx
data_table.Investigations: Bedside blood glucose 5.2 mmol/L, no other tests indicated by guideline; viral upper respiratory tract infection clinically
data_table.Medications: Paracetamol 15 mg/kg given at home 90 minutes ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management for this child and her family?

options.text: Admit for inpatient electroencephalogram and magnetic resonance imaging of the brain this admission
options.rationale: Routine EEG and MRI are not indicated after simple febrile seizures even when recurrent; investigations are reserved for focal, prolonged, or atypical seizures. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizures)

options.text: Discharge with parental seizure education, antipyretic-as-needed advice, and rectal diazepam only for prolonged events
options.rationale: Simple febrile seizures, even recurrent, do not need investigations, anti-epileptic prophylaxis, or routine antipyretic prophylaxis (which does not prevent recurrence). Education on safe positioning, timing the seizure, and rescue rectal diazepam if a seizure exceeds 5 minutes is the AU standard. Long-term epilepsy risk is only modestly increased; reassurance is appropriate. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizures; Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Commence regular oral sodium valproate prophylaxis until age five for the recurrent febrile seizures
options.rationale: Valproate prophylaxis is not used for febrile seizures; the small benefit is outweighed by adverse effects and the natural history is benign in simple cases. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Prescribe alternating paracetamol and ibuprofen at the first sign of fever to prevent further events
options.rationale: Antipyretics improve comfort but do not reduce febrile seizure recurrence; routine alternating dosing risks medication error and adverse effects. (Source: RCH CPG: Fever in children)

options.text: Refer for routine outpatient paediatric neurology review and consider rectal diazepam prophylaxis daily
options.rationale: Routine neurology referral is unnecessary for simple recurrent febrile seizures; daily rectal diazepam prophylaxis is not evidence-based. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizures)

explanation.summary: Simple febrile seizures are generalised, last under 15 minutes, do not recur within 24 hours, and occur in a child aged 6 months to 6 years with fever from a clear extracranial source. Recurrence happens in around 30%, and the long-term risk of epilepsy is only modestly above background. Investigations, anti-epileptic prophylaxis, and routine antipyretic prophylaxis do not change outcomes. Parental education on positioning, timing, and rescue medication for seizures longer than 5 minutes is the cornerstone of care.
key_points: Simple febrile seizure: 6 months to 6 years, generalised, under 15 minutes, no recurrence in 24 hours
No investigations or anti-epileptic prophylaxis - even after recurrence
Parental education and rescue plan for seizures over 5 minutes
Refer if focal features, prolonged, recurrence within 24 hours, or atypical age
explanation.pearls: Rectal diazepam 0.5 mg/kg or buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg is the AU rescue dose if any seizure lasts longer than 5 minutes - this is the prescription on discharge, not daily prophylaxis.

stem:
A 5-year-old boy is brought to your general practice clinic with daily soiling for the past 3 months. His father says 'he poos into his underpants without realising, and he hasn't sat on the toilet for a proper poo for at least 6 weeks'. Hard stools have been infrequent. He started school recently and avoids the school toilets. Diet is variable, fluid intake adequate, and growth has tracked the 50th centile throughout.

There is no blood per rectum, no vomiting, no abdominal distension at infancy, and meconium passed within 24 hours of birth. On examination he is afebrile with a soft, non-tender, palpably loaded left iliac fossa and faecal mass extending across to the right. Spine, anal position, sacral and lower limb neurological examination are normal. Recent abdominal X-ray taken at the emergency department demonstrates heavy faecal loading throughout the colon without evidence of obstruction.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 96, BP 100/62 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, palpable faecal mass left iliac fossa extending to right, normal perianal exam, normal anal position, normal lower limb tone and reflexes, normal sacral skin
data_table.Investigations: Abdominal X-ray heavy faecal loading throughout colon, no air-fluid levels, no obstruction

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer for paediatric surgical opinion regarding suspected Hirschsprung disease and contrast enema studies
options.rationale: Hirschsprung typically presents in the first months of life with delayed meconium and a tight rectum on examination; this boy passed meconium normally and has overflow encopresis after age 3 - features against Hirschsprung. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Recommend dietary fibre and fluid changes alone with general practitioner review in four weeks
options.rationale: Lifestyle measures alone do not clear established faecal impaction; disimpaction is required before maintenance and behavioural work can succeed. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Commence high-dose oral macrogol disimpaction over three days then a maintenance dose, plus toilet sit routine
options.rationale: Functional constipation with overflow soiling and palpable faecal mass requires disimpaction first. Oral macrogol with electrolytes escalated over three days then continued at maintenance is the AU first-line, paired with a sit routine after meals, a star chart, and de-shaming the soiling. Stimulant laxatives are added if macrogol alone fails. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation; Therapeutic Guidelines: Gastrointestinal)

options.text: Commence regular daily phosphate enemas as outpatient treatment for the next two weeks at home
options.rationale: Phosphate enemas risk hyperphosphataemia and hypocalcaemia in children and are not first-line outpatient therapy; oral macrogol is preferred. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Investigate with thyroid function tests, coeliac serology, and serum calcium before any treatment is started
options.rationale: Functional constipation is a clinical diagnosis without red flags; routine endocrine and coeliac screening is reserved for growth faltering, failure to respond to standard treatment, or other clinical concern. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

explanation.summary: Childhood functional constipation with overflow encopresis is managed in two phases: disimpaction (high-dose macrogol over a few days) and maintenance (daily macrogol with toilet behaviour work). De-shaming the soiling and a structured sit routine after meals reframes the family understanding. Stimulant laxatives are added for refractory cases. Red flags (delayed meconium, ribbon stool from birth, neurological signs, faltering growth) prompt referral for Hirschsprung or other organic disease.
key_points: Disimpaction must precede maintenance laxative use
Oral macrogol with electrolytes is first-line; phosphate enemas avoided
Behavioural sit routine after meals plus de-shaming the soiling is essential
Hirschsprung red flags: delayed meconium, tight rectum, neonatal obstruction
explanation.pearls: Oral macrogol with electrolytes (e.g. Movicol) at the AU paediatric disimpaction schedule clears most impactions within three to five days before transitioning to a maintenance dose.

stem:
You are the paediatric surgical registrar called to assess a term 40-hour-old boy born to non-consanguineous parents. The midwife reports 'he hasn't passed meconium yet and his tummy looks puffier this afternoon'. He has had two non-bilious vomits but is feeding poorly. There is no family history of cystic fibrosis. Antenatal scans were unremarkable; karyotype not performed.

On examination he has visible abdominal distension and reduced bowel sounds. Anal position is normal and the anocutaneous reflex is present, but per-rectal examination yields a snug rectum and an explosive passage of meconium after gentle finger withdrawal. He has no obvious dysmorphism. Abdominal X-ray shows distended gas-filled small and large bowel loops to a transition point at the rectosigmoid level with collapsed distal rectum. Newborn screening swab is pending. He is haemodynamically stable.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 148, BP 64/40 mmHg, RR 50, SpO2 97% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Abdominal distension, reduced bowel sounds, snug rectum with explosive passage of meconium after rectal examination, normal anal position, no dysmorphism
data_table.Investigations: Abdominal X-ray with multiple distended bowel loops, rectosigmoid transition zone, collapsed distal rectum; FBC, electrolytes, blood gas pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Rectal suction biopsy with histological assessment for ganglion cells and acetylcholinesterase staining
options.rationale: Hirschsprung disease is suspected from delayed meconium passage, abdominal distension, the explosive 'squirt sign' after rectal examination, and rectosigmoid transition zone on X-ray. Rectal suction biopsy demonstrating absent ganglion cells with hypertrophied nerve trunks and increased acetylcholinesterase activity is the diagnostic gold standard before staged pull-through. Contrast enema can support but is not diagnostic and can miss ultra-short segment disease. (Source: ANZAPS Hirschsprung consensus; RCH CPG: Neonatal bowel obstruction)

options.text: Water-soluble contrast enema looking for a string sign and microcolon in the descending colon
options.rationale: Contrast enema findings of microcolon are characteristic of meconium ileus from cystic fibrosis - not Hirschsprung where a transition zone with proximal dilatation is seen. The string sign is a small bowel pattern, not a Hirschsprung feature. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal bowel obstruction)

options.text: Urgent anorectal manometry to assess the rectoanal inhibitory reflex in the neonatal intensive care unit
options.rationale: Manometry detects the absent rectoanal inhibitory reflex but is technically difficult and unreliable in the neonatal period; suction biopsy is the AU standard in newborns. (Source: ANZAPS Hirschsprung consensus)

options.text: Plain abdominal ultrasound to characterise bowel wall thickness at the suspected transition zone level
options.rationale: Ultrasound does not characterise mural innervation; it cannot diagnose aganglionosis and is not part of the diagnostic algorithm. (Source: ANZAPS Hirschsprung consensus)

options.text: Sweat chloride testing on a properly weighted sample collected in the second week of life as next step
options.rationale: Sweat testing assesses cystic fibrosis-related meconium ileus, not Hirschsprung; antenatal scans, no family history, and the squirt sign with rectosigmoid transition point do not fit. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

explanation.summary: Hirschsprung disease (congenital aganglionosis of the distal bowel) typically presents in the first 48 hours of life with delayed meconium, abdominal distension, and an explosive rectal squirt after digital examination. A rectosigmoid transition zone is the commonest site; long-segment and total colonic aganglionosis are rarer. Rectal suction biopsy is the diagnostic gold standard, followed by definitive pull-through surgery once enterocolitis is excluded. Enterocolitis (sepsis, severe distension, explosive diarrhoea) is the principal short-term mortality risk and demands rectal washouts, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and decompression.
key_points: Delayed meconium beyond 48 hours plus distension plus squirt sign = suspect Hirschsprung
Rectal suction biopsy is the gold-standard neonatal diagnostic test
Anorectal manometry is unreliable under 1 month; not first-line
Hirschsprung enterocolitis is the principal short-term mortality risk
explanation.pearls: Rectal suction biopsy shows absent ganglion cells in the submucosal plexus with hypertrophied unmyelinated nerve trunks; acetylcholinesterase staining or calretinin loss confirms the diagnosis.

subtopic_detail: HPV not 16-18 with LSIL reflex cytology
stem:
A 28-year-old woman attends your general practice clinic for results of her routine 5-yearly Cervical Screening Test. She has no abnormal bleeding, no postcoital bleeding, and no pelvic pain. She had a previous screening test 5 years ago that was negative. She is sexually active with a single male partner of 3 years, uses combined oral contraception, and has had no abnormal cervical results in the past. She is not pregnant.

Her current Cervical Screening Test reports oncogenic HPV detected (non-16, non-18 type) with reflex liquid-based cytology showing low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (possible LSIL). Speculum at sample-taking was unremarkable; the cervix appeared smooth and there were no contact-bleeding findings noted. She is up to date with her HPV vaccination history (3-dose schedule in adolescence). She asks 'do I need a biopsy now?'.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72, BP 116/74 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Speculum: cervix smooth, no friable lesion, no contact bleeding, no abnormal discharge
data_table.Investigations: Cervical Screening Test: HPV non-16/18 detected; reflex liquid-based cytology low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (possible LSIL)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Repeat the Cervical Screening Test in 12 months irrespective of cytology grade or HPV genotype reported
options.rationale: Twelve-month repeat applies after HPV non-16/18 with negative or low-grade reflex cytology in a routine intermediate-risk pathway; the description fits but the discriminator is the symptom set and prior negative test. This option phrasing is incomplete because it omits the 12-month and 24-month consecutive logic; the official program ties the management to the cytology grade. (Source: NCSP Guidelines)

options.text: Refer to a gynaecologist for diagnostic excisional biopsy (large loop excision of the transformation zone) within 6 weeks
options.rationale: Excisional procedures are reserved for biopsy-confirmed high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (HSIL) or higher; LSIL on screening cytology with non-16/18 HPV does not warrant immediate excision. (Source: NCSP Guidelines)

options.text: Refer urgently to colposcopy clinic for diagnostic assessment and directed cervical biopsies within 8 weeks
options.rationale: Direct colposcopy is the AU intermediate-risk referral pathway only after a second HPV non-16/18 with persistent low-grade abnormality at 12 months, or any reflex HSIL result. A first-occasion HPV non-16/18 with possible LSIL is managed by 12-month repeat. (Source: NCSP Guidelines)

options.text: Treat her empirically with vaginal imiquimod and repeat the Cervical Screening Test in 3 months
options.rationale: Topical imiquimod is not part of the AU screening program management; empirical treatment without histological diagnosis is not recommended. (Source: NCSP Guidelines)

options.text: Repeat the Cervical Screening Test in 12 months and refer to colposcopy if HPV remains detected at that point
options.rationale: An HPV non-16/18 result with reflex possible LSIL or LSIL is intermediate risk under the AU NCSP and is managed by repeat Cervical Screening Test at 12 months. If HPV (any oncogenic type) is still detected at the 12-month repeat, colposcopy referral is required regardless of cytology. If the 12-month repeat is HPV-negative, she returns to 5-yearly screening. (Source: NCSP Guidelines)

explanation.summary: The Australian National Cervical Screening Program uses primary HPV testing every 5 years from age 25 to 74. HPV 16/18 detection triggers direct colposcopy referral; HPV non-16/18 results are stratified by reflex cytology. Negative or low-grade reflex cytology with HPV non-16/18 leads to a 12-month repeat test (intermediate risk pathway). HSIL or higher on reflex cytology, or persistent HPV at 12-month repeat, triggers colposcopy. Self-collection from age 25 is available for under-screened women.
key_points: HPV 16/18: direct colposcopy regardless of cytology
HPV non-16/18 with negative or low-grade cytology: 12-month repeat
Persistent HPV (any type) at 12-month repeat: colposcopy
Self-collection available from age 25 for under-screened women
explanation.pearls: AU intermediate-risk pathway: HPV non-16/18 with negative or low-grade reflex cytology returns at 12 months; if still HPV-positive at that visit, colposcopy is the next step regardless of cytology grade.

stem:
A 32-year-old woman attends your general practice clinic 4 months postpartum complaining of palpitations, tremor, weight loss of 4 kg despite a good appetite, and feeling 'hot all the time'. Her mother says 'she has been tearful and snappy, she's not herself'. She is breastfeeding and has not had a postnatal contraceptive review. Her last menstrual period before pregnancy was regular. She denies eye pain, double vision, or visual changes.

On examination she has a fine tremor of outstretched hands, a smooth non-tender symmetrical small goitre without bruit, and no proptosis or lid lag. There is no pretibial myxoedema. Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale score is 9. Initial bloods show TSH 0.04 mIU/L (0.5-4.0), free T4 28 pmol/L (10-22), and free T3 9 pmol/L (3.5-6.5). She wonders 'is it just my hormones from feeding?'.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 108 regular, BP 124/76 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.9 °C
data_table.Examination: Fine tremor, smooth small symmetrical goitre, no bruit, no proptosis or lid lag, no pretibial myxoedema, lactating
data_table.Investigations: TSH 0.04 mIU/L (0.5-4.0), free T4 28 pmol/L (10-22), free T3 9 pmol/L (3.5-6.5), EPDS 9; TSH-receptor antibody pending, thyroid uptake scan not yet performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation to clarify the cause?

options.text: Twenty-four-hour urinary iodine excretion testing before considering any further investigation steps
options.rationale: Iodine excretion testing assesses iodine status at a population level and does not differentiate thyroiditis from Graves disease in an individual. (Source: ATA Postpartum Thyroiditis Guideline)

options.text: Repeat thyroid function tests in 4 weeks without any further immediate biochemical or imaging testing
options.rationale: Both postpartum thyroiditis and Graves can persist or progress; differentiation needs to happen now because antithyroid drug therapy is appropriate only for Graves and not for transient thyroiditis. (Source: ATA Postpartum Thyroiditis Guideline)

options.text: Refer for radioactive iodine uptake scanning this week to differentiate Graves from thyroiditis
options.rationale: Radioactive iodine is contraindicated in breastfeeding women; a technetium scan with milk withholding could be used but is not the first-line discriminator. (Source: ATA Postpartum Thyroiditis Guideline; AMH)

options.text: Order TSH-receptor antibody and TPO antibody titres in addition to a small thyroid ultrasound now
options.rationale: TSH-receptor antibody (TRAb) is the key discriminator: positive supports Graves disease; negative with TPO antibody positivity supports postpartum thyroiditis. Ultrasound shows diffuse hypervascularity in Graves and patchy hypoechogenicity in thyroiditis. TRAb-positive results steer the patient toward antithyroid drug therapy with breastfeeding-compatible carbimazole at low dose; TRAb-negative directs symptomatic management. (Source: ATA Postpartum Thyroiditis Guideline; RANZCOG)

options.text: Commence empirical carbimazole 15 mg orally daily for biochemical hyperthyroidism while awaiting any further work
options.rationale: Empirical antithyroid drug therapy is wrong if the cause is postpartum thyroiditis (a destructive process not responsive to antithyroid medication) and exposes a breastfeeding mother unnecessarily; discriminate first. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Postpartum thyroiditis affects up to 5% of women within 12 months of delivery and is a destructive autoimmune process producing transient hyperthyroidism followed by hypothyroidism and recovery in most cases. Graves disease in the postpartum period is autoimmune-stimulatory and persists. TRAb positivity, diffuse hypervascular goitre on ultrasound, and ophthalmopathy point to Graves. TPO antibody positivity with normal vascularity and a transient course points to thyroiditis. Carbimazole is the AU breastfeeding-compatible antithyroid drug at low dose; propylthiouracil reserved for first trimester or carbimazole intolerance.
key_points: TRAb is the key discriminator: positive = Graves, negative = thyroiditis
Radioactive iodine uptake contraindicated in breastfeeding women
Carbimazole low-dose is the AU breastfeeding-compatible antithyroid agent
Postpartum thyroiditis usually self-resolves; surveillance for permanent hypothyroidism
explanation.pearls: Breastfeeding-compatible carbimazole up to 20 mg per day is supported by AMH where serum testing in the infant has been used and infant thyroid function followed at intervals; propylthiouracil is preferred only for first trimester.
